events.title: United 2026 Bid Formally Announced
events.detail: The United States Soccer Federation, Canada Soccer, and the Mexican Football Federation jointly announced the "United 2026" bid for the FIFA World Cup, marking the first tri-nation hosting proposal in the tournament's history. The bid positioned the joint hosting model as a logistical and commercial breakthrough.

events.title: FIFA Awards 2026 Hosting Rights to United Bid
events.detail: At the 68th FIFA Congress in Moscow, the joint USA/Canada/Mexico bid won the right to host the 2026 FIFA World Cup, defeating Morocco 134 votes to 65. The decision followed controversy over FIFA's anti-corruption framework and concerns about the Moroccan bid's readiness.

events.title: FIFA Confirms 48-Team Expanded Format
events.detail: The FIFA Council unanimously approved the expansion of the World Cup from 32 to 48 teams beginning with the 2026 edition. The new format introduces 12 groups of four teams, with the top two from each group plus the eight best third-place finishers advancing to a Round of 32. The change adds 40 matches and extends the tournament by 7 days.

events.title: FIFA Announces All 16 Official Host Cities
events.detail: FIFA officially confirmed all 16 host cities for the 2026 World Cup: 11 in the United States (Atlanta, Boston, Dallas, Houston, Kansas City, Los Angeles, Miami, New York/New Jersey, Philadelphia, San Francisco Bay Area, Seattle), 3 in Mexico (Guadalajara, Mexico City, Monterrey), and 2 in Canada (Toronto, Vancouver). The announcement confirmed MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, NJ as the Final venue.

events.title: Official Tournament Brand Identity Unveiled
events.detail: FIFA unveiled the official visual identity, emblem, and branding for the 2026 World Cup. The emblem features a stylized trophy design incorporating North American cultural elements representing all three host nations. The identity was revealed in a global ceremony broadcast from Los Angeles.

events.title: Official Match Schedule Framework Released
events.detail: FIFA released the framework match schedule for the 2026 World Cup, confirming tournament dates, venue rotation plans, and the structure of travel clusters. The US will host 78 of 104 matches; Mexico and Canada will each host 13. All quarterfinals, semifinals, and the Final will be played at US venues.

events.title: MetLife Stadium Confirmed as World Cup Final Venue
events.detail: FIFA officially confirmed MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey — the New York Giants and Jets NFL home — as the venue for the 2026 World Cup Final on July 19, 2026. With a capacity of 82,500, it will be the largest venue used in a World Cup since Brazil's Maracanã in 2014. AT&T Stadium (Dallas) and Mercedes-Benz Stadium (Atlanta) were named semi-final venues.

events.title: SoFi Stadium (LA) Confirmed for US Opening Match
events.detail: SoFi Stadium in Inglewood, California — the home of the LA Rams and Chargers — was confirmed as the venue for the United States' opening group stage match on June 12, 2026. The $5.5 billion stadium, opened in 2020, is also slated to host the Opening and Closing Ceremonies of the 2028 Los Angeles Olympic Games.

events.title: Estadio Azteca Set for World Cup Opening Match
events.detail: FIFA confirmed Estadio Azteca in Mexico City as the venue for the tournament's opening match on June 11, 2026 — giving Mexico the honor of kickstarting the first 48-team World Cup. The historic stadium, which previously hosted 1970 and 1986 World Cup finals, underwent renovations to meet modern FIFA standards and safety requirements.

events.title: CONCACAF League Qualifying Phase Begins
events.detail: The CONCACAF qualifying process for non-host nations kicked off with the league phase. CONCACAF allocated 6 berths for the region (beyond the automatic host spots for USA, Mexico, and Canada), with additional intercontinental playoff paths available. The expanded format created high stakes across the Caribbean and Central American nations.

events.title: Argentina Clinches CONMEBOL Top Spot with Record Points
events.detail: Defending World Cup champions Argentina topped the CONMEBOL qualifying table with a record 38 points, confirming their place at the 2026 tournament. Brazil, Colombia, Uruguay, Ecuador, and Paraguay also secured automatic qualification. Bolivia qualified for the intercontinental playoff as the seventh-place team.

events.title: AFC Sends 8 Teams: Japan, South Korea, Iran, Australia, Jordan, Uzbekistan, Saudi Arabia, Qatar
events.detail: Asia's qualifying process concluded with eight direct qualifiers: Japan, Iran, South Korea, Jordan, Australia, and Uzbekistan from the third round, plus Saudi Arabia and Qatar through the fourth round. Iraq advanced to the intercontinental playoff as Asia's remaining contender. All four major Asian football powers earned direct berths.

events.title: Scotland Qualifies for First World Cup Since 1998
events.detail: Scotland secured qualification for the 2026 FIFA World Cup, ending a 28-year absence from the tournament since France 1998. The achievement was celebrated as a watershed moment for Scottish football, capping a significant improvement under national team management and qualifying through the UEFA zone.

events.title: Nine African Nations Qualify: CAF Allocation Expanded
events.detail: Africa's expanded allocation of 9+1 spots was filled, with Algeria, Cape Verde, Egypt, Ghana, Ivory Coast, Morocco, Senegal, South Africa, and Tunisia qualifying directly. Cape Verde made history as a first-time qualifier. DR Congo earned an intercontinental playoff spot by winning a decisive CAF playoff tournament ahead of Nigeria, Gabon, and Cameroon.

events.title: Haiti, Panama, Curaçao Qualify; Curaçao Makes History
events.detail: CONCACAF's three direct qualifying spots (beyond the hosts) were claimed by Haiti, Panama, and Curaçao. Curaçao's qualification was historic — the island nation became the smallest country ever to qualify for a FIFA World Cup by population. Jamaica and Suriname advanced to the intercontinental playoff.

events.title: UEFA Direct Qualifiers Confirmed: England, France, Germany, Spain Lead 12-Team Bloc
events.detail: Twelve European nations secured direct World Cup qualification: England, France, Germany, Spain, Portugal, Netherlands, Belgium, Switzerland, Austria, Norway, Croatia, and Scotland. Four additional European berths remained to be decided by playoff matches in March 2026. Italy — notably — failed to qualify automatically and faced a playoff.

events.title: New Zealand Qualifies as OFC's Direct Representative
events.detail: New Zealand secured Oceania's direct World Cup berth, qualifying for their sixth World Cup. New Caledonia also advanced from OFC to participate in the intercontinental playoff, giving Oceania a chance for a second representative at the 2026 tournament.

events.title: FIFA Relocates Final Draw from Las Vegas to Washington D.C.
events.detail: FIFA moved the Final Draw venue from Las Vegas (the originally planned location) to the Kennedy Center in Washington, D.C., reportedly at the request of President Donald Trump. The change drew criticism from observers who argued it gave the US President an undue platform at a sporting event. FIFA officials cited the prestige of the Washington venue.

events.title: Final Draw Held at Kennedy Center — 12 Groups Revealed
events.detail: FIFA held the official Final Draw at the Kennedy Center in Washington, D.C., with Presidents Trump, Sheinbaum, and Canadian PM Carney opening the first ballot. All 48 teams were drawn into 12 groups (A–L). Key matchups included Mexico vs. South Africa (Group A, opening match), USA vs. Paraguay and Australia (Group D), and England vs. Croatia (Group L). France (Group I) and Argentina (Group J) were drawn into opposite pathways.

events.title: FIFA Awards Trump Inaugural 'Peace Prize' at Draw Ceremony
events.detail: At the Final Draw, FIFA President Gianni Infantino presented Donald Trump with FIFA's inaugural 'Peace Prize,' described as recognition for his 'exceptional and extraordinary actions to promote peace and unity around the world.' The award was created without formal FIFA Council approval and drew immediate criticism from human rights organizations, journalists, and football governance experts who questioned FIFA's independence.

events.title: Full 104-Match Schedule Published
events.detail: One day after the Final Draw, FIFA released the complete 104-match schedule for all venues and dates. The opening match was confirmed as Mexico vs. South Africa at Estadio Azteca on June 11. The US opens June 12 at SoFi Stadium vs. Paraguay; Canada opens June 12 at BMO Field. The Final is set for MetLife Stadium on July 19, 2026.

events.title: Amnesty and HRW Issue Pre-Tournament Human Rights Warnings
events.detail: Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch published coordinated reports warning FIFA and host governments about human rights risks at the 2026 World Cup. Key concerns included US immigration enforcement near venues, LGBTQ+ safety in restrictive states, workers' rights under anti-union federal policies, and press freedom risks. Forty-six civil society organizations signed an open letter demanding action before kickoff.

events.title: Intercontinental Playoff Bracket Set: Guadalajara and Monterrey Host
events.detail: FIFA confirmed the intercontinental playoff bracket and venues for March 2026. Six teams — Jamaica, New Caledonia, Suriname, Bolivia, DR Congo, and Iraq — compete in two three-team mini-tournaments hosted in Guadalajara and Monterrey. The top two teams (one from each bracket) earn the final two World Cup berths. Semifinals play March 26 and finals March 31.

events.title: UEFA Playoff Paths Confirmed: Italy, Wales, Ukraine, Poland Among Contenders
events.detail: The four remaining European World Cup spots were set to be decided in March 2026 playoffs. Path A: Italy vs. Northern Ireland (semi), Wales vs. Bosnia-Herzegovina (semi), with the winners meeting in the final. Path B: Ukraine vs. Sweden (semi), Poland vs. Albania (semi). Italy — three-time World Cup champions — faced the prospect of a third consecutive playoff elimination.

events.title: Sport & Rights Alliance Issues Comprehensive Pre-Tournament Warning
events.detail: The Sport & Rights Alliance — comprising Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, the ACLU, and Transparency International — published a comprehensive report warning that the 2026 World Cup faces an 'unprecedented convergence of human rights challenges.' The report detailed concerns over US anti-union federal policies exposing workers to exploitation, corruption risks in host nations, press freedom restrictions, and FIFA's inadequate enforcement of its own 2016 Human Rights Policy.

events.title: Intercontinental Playoff Semifinals: Bolivia Beat Suriname 2–1; Jamaica Beat New Caledonia 1–0
events.detail: The FIFA intercontinental playoff semifinals produced clear results on March 26, 2026. Bolivia defeated Suriname 2–1 at Estadio BBVA in Monterrey (Paniagua 72', Terceros pen 79') to advance to the Pathway 2 final vs Iraq on March 31. Jamaica defeated New Caledonia 1–0 at Estadio Akron in Guadalajara (Bailey Cadamarteri) to advance to the Pathway 1 final vs DR Congo. The two finals on March 31 determine the last two World Cup berths in the 48-team field.

events.title: UEFA Playoff Semifinals: Eight Nations Advance — Kosovo Stun Slovakia 4–3, Gyökeres Hat-trick for Sweden
events.detail: All eight UEFA World Cup playoff semifinals were played on March 26, producing dramatic results. Italy beat Northern Ireland 2–0 (Path A, Tonali + Kean). Bosnia-Herzegovina eliminated Wales on penalties after a 1–1 AET draw (Path A). Sweden thrashed Ukraine 3–0 via a Viktor Gyökeres hat-trick (Path B). Poland defeated Albania 2–1 (Path B). Turkey edged Romania 1–0 through Kadioglu 53' (Güler assist) (Path C). Kosovo stunned Slovakia 4–3 in the day's biggest upset (Path C). Denmark routed North Macedonia 4–0 (Path D). Czechia beat Republic of Ireland 4–2 on penalties after 2–2 AET (Path D). All four Path finals are set for March 31.

events.title: FIFA Opens Last-Minute Ticket Sales Phase from April 1
events.detail: FIFA announced on March 26 that a final last-minute ticket sales phase for the 2026 World Cup will begin April 1 on a first-come, first-served basis. Over one million tickets were sold in prior windows amid unprecedented demand — more than 500 million applications received. The announcement follows sustained fan and player backlash over pricing, with Final tickets reaching $143,750 on FIFA's official resale platform.

events.title: Iran Issues Formal Travel Ban to 'Hostile' Countries — World Cup Crisis Escalates
events.detail: Iran's Ministry of Sports issued a formal ban on March 27 prohibiting all Iranian national and club teams from traveling to countries classified as 'hostile,' stating: 'The presence of national and club teams in countries that are considered hostile and are unable to ensure the security of Iranian athletes and team members is prohibited until further notice.' While triggered by a club football scheduling dispute, the order directly blocks Iran's three World Cup Group G matches — all scheduled at US venues (Los Angeles and Seattle). Iran's ambassador in Mexico City was reported negotiating with FIFA to relocate matches to Mexico; FIFA again rejected the request. Iran's sports minister had declared in March that Iran 'under no circumstances' could participate in the US. Six Iranian women's national team players reportedly sought asylum in Australia earlier in March after refusing to sing the national anthem.

events.title: USMNT 2–5 Belgium — Pre-World Cup Alarm After Atlanta Rout
events.detail: The United States Men's National Team suffered a 2–5 defeat to Belgium (FIFA 9th) at Mercedes-Benz Stadium in Atlanta (att. 66,867). Belgium outshot the US 21–12. Weston McKennie opened the scoring (39', Antonee Robinson assist) but Belgium dominated after halftime through Debast, Onana, De Ketelaere (pen) and a Lukébakio brace. Agyemang pulled one back (87'). Coach Pochettino said the team failed to 'keep the intensity.' The USMNT debuted new 'Stars and Stripes' Nike kits. Portugal follow on March 31, the last match before the May 26 roster announcement.

events.title: Amnesty 'Humanity Must Win' Report: World Cup Risks 'Stage for Repression'
events.detail: Amnesty International published its major pre-tournament report warning the 2026 World Cup risks becoming a 'stage for repression.' The report cited a 'human rights emergency' in the US under the Trump administration — mass deportations, ICE enforcement near stadiums, LGBTQ+ rollbacks — and called on FIFA and all three host governments to take urgent protective action. With $11 billion at stake and 73 days to kickoff, FIFA has not secured any formal moratorium on immigration enforcement near venues.

events.title: Six World Cup Finals on March 31 — Full 48-Team Field Confirmed
events.detail: All remaining World Cup berths were decided on March 31 in six simultaneous finals. UEFA results: Bosnia-Herzegovina beat Italy on penalties (Group B); Sweden beat Poland 3–0 (Group F); Kosovo beat Turkey 4–1 (Group D — joins USA, Paraguay, Australia); Denmark beat Czechia (Group A). FIFA Intercontinental results: DR Congo beat Jamaica 1–0 AET via Axel Tuanzebe (Group K — Portugal, Colombia, Uzbekistan); Iraq beat Bolivia 2–1 (Group I — France, Senegal, Norway). Italy missed the World Cup for a third straight time. Kosovo qualified for their first-ever World Cup. DR Congo returned after 52 years. Iraq qualified despite extraordinary overland travel to Monterrey due to regional airspace closures.

events.title: USMNT 0–2 Portugal — Back-to-Back Losses Raise Pre-World Cup Alarm
events.detail: The USMNT lost 0–2 to Portugal at Mercedes-Benz Stadium in Atlanta (att. 72,297). Goals from Francisco Trincão (37') and João Félix (59') exposed recurring defensive weaknesses. Portugal played without Ronaldo, Leão, and Diogo Costa. Combined with the 2–5 loss to Belgium on March 28, the US finished March camp 0–2 with a 2–7 aggregate — Pochettino's worst camp ahead of the home World Cup. The 26-man World Cup roster is due May 26; next friendly is May 31 vs Senegal in Charlotte.

events.title: FIFA Last-Minute Ticket Sales Open — Website Glitch Chaos on April 2
events.detail: FIFA's final ticket sales phase opened April 1 at 11:00 AM ET, first-come, first-served, amid sustained fan anger over pricing. On April 2, a technical error routed buyers into the wrong queue for 90+ minutes. US Congress members had formally written calling the tournament 'the most financially exclusionary ever.' The cheapest openly available final ticket starts at $2,790; the FIFA official resale platform reopened April 2 with secondary prices reaching $143,750.

events.title: FIFA Confirms Iran's World Cup Participation — Congress Decision April 30
events.detail: After attending Iran's 5–0 win over Costa Rica in Antalya on March 31, FIFA President Infantino publicly confirmed: 'Iran will be at the World Cup.' Iran's Football Federation VP affirmed FIFA compliance. But Iran's Sports Minister had declared Iran 'under no circumstances' can participate. The FIFA Congress in Vancouver on April 30 is the next decision point for contingency planning if the conflict continues. Iran's Group G matches remain at US venues: vs New Zealand (LA, June 15), vs Belgium (LA, June 21), vs Egypt (Seattle, June 26).

events.title: FSE & Euroconsumers File EU Commission Complaint Against FIFA Over Ticket Prices
events.detail: Football Supporters Europe (FSE) and Euroconsumers filed a formal complaint with the European Commission on April 3 alleging FIFA abused its dominant market position to impose excessive ticket prices for the 2026 World Cup. The complaint cites Final ticket face values reaching $10,990 (up from $8,680) with secondary prices hitting $143,750 — 41 times face value — on FIFA's own resale platform. The organizations called for an EU competition law investigation. The complaint follows a formal US Congress letter calling the tournament 'the most financially exclusionary ever' and the April 2 website glitch that left fans in the wrong queue for 90+ minutes. FIFA has not publicly responded.

events.title: USMNT Calls 27 Players for March Camp — Belgium and Portugal Friendlies Before World Cup
events.detail: US head coach Mauricio Pochettino named a 27-player squad for the March 2026 camp, with the USMNT playing Belgium (March 28) and Portugal (March 31) in Atlanta at Mercedes-Benz Stadium — the final major test matches before the 26-player World Cup roster is announced. Notable returns include defender Antonee Robinson (back from injury after missing all of 2025 international duty) and Chris Richards (named 2025 US Soccer Male Player of the Year). The USMNT debuted new Stars and Stripes kits at these fixtures.

events.title: Iran Sports Minister: Participation 'Very Low' Without Security Guarantees — Relocation Request Still Valid
events.detail: Iran Sports Minister Ahmad Donyamali told Anadolu Agency on April 5 that Iran's relocation request 'is still valid, but we have not yet received a response.' He offered the government's first conditional opening: 'If the relevant security guarantees are provided, our government will decide on Iran's participation.' Bloomberg analysis on April 6 concluded that relocating Iran's matches is 'extraordinarily difficult' and without modern historical precedent, citing FIFA host city agreements, broadcast contracts, and ticket allocations all structured around fixed venues. FIFA President Infantino maintains 'there is no Plan B, C, or D.' The disconnect between Iran's football federation (willing to compete) and its government (opposing participation at US venues) remains unresolved 24 days before the April 30 FIFA Congress in Vancouver — the formal decision point.

events.title: Serge Gnabry Rules Himself Out of World Cup — Germany's Injury Crisis Deepens
events.detail: German forward Serge Gnabry confirmed via Instagram on April 22 that his World Cup dream is 'sadly over' after an adductor muscle tear in his right leg sustained during Bayern Munich training. Expected recovery is approximately three months — ruling him out of Germany's Group E campaign opener vs. Curaçao on June 14 at Estadio Azteca. Gnabry (30, 44 caps) was a key attacker under Julian Nagelsmann, who now faces a forward line dependent on Florian Wirtz, Jamal Musiala, and Kai Havertz. Germany's squad concerns are compounded by Leroy Sané's ongoing injury doubts. Group E: Germany vs. Curaçao (June 14, Mexico City), vs. Ivory Coast (June 18, Kansas City), vs. Ecuador (June 23, Boston).

events.title: Rubio Clears Iran Players for World Cup — But Bars IRGC-Linked Personnel; Taremi's Visa Under Scrutiny
events.detail: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio stated on April 24 that Iran's players may attend the 2026 World Cup — 'Nothing from the US has told them they can't come' — but drew a hard line: anyone with ties to the IRGC (designated by Washington as a foreign terrorist organization) will be denied entry. 'They can't bring a bunch of IRGC terrorists into our country and pretend they are journalists and athletic trainers,' Rubio said. President Trump added his administration 'would not want to affect the athletes.' The statement raised urgent concern about Iran's squad: Iran captain Mehdi Taremi (Inter Milan) served at an IRGC Navy base in Bushehr 2010–2012; multiple other squad members also completed mandatory IRGC service. Iran's Football Federation president Mehdi Taj confirmed replacement players have been identified for those denied visas. The statement is the clearest direct US government position on Iran participation and came exactly 6 days before the FIFA Congress in Vancouver on April 30 — the formal decision point.
